speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This conversation with Byers Sanderson took place at his home near Bovill on January 23, 1976. The interviewer is Sam Schrager.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Powell down here to Princeton. I told him I wanted to cruise the timber and I had this timber that I could buy and I says Mr. Stout of Genesee owns the horse ranch and I says he's got a thousand or so acres down in there and there's lots of timber on it. I says I want to buy it. Or buy the timber. I was logging at that time. I had the logging crew. I had two cats and a loader and the logging equipment, chainsaws, and I had probably half a million feet of timber out here on the horse ranch, off of the horse ranch.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: I wanted to buy it. Well, I told him I had to have Mr. Powell, the ranger station, come up and he was an old cruiser and I says he'll go through and give you an actual estimate of your timber. Well that was Friday, Monday morning. And Powell went right down to the old fellow and told him that the government was buying all this timber that they could get ahold of. And he says I'll go through and cruise it and we'll buy it. Oh yeah. Well, Stott says I told Mr. Sanderson that he could have the timber and he says if he don't fit the timber then he says I'll deal with you if you want it. But he says I won't carry it over his head because I told him and he says I figure my word is trustworthy. So the next morning Mr. Tout came up to the house and he says buyers, he says the forestry come up there and wanted to buy that timber. I told him they couldn't have it and I says I give you first chance at it and if you want it we'll go through and we'll make the deal.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And so I said well I was working at the shop and I had the crew out onto the side. I work in this other ground and I says I'll be over this week sometime and I'll get papers fixed up and we'll make a deal.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And I went over to the courthouse. Well there was 160 acres up here that I'd cruised and then over on the other side there was another big bunch of timber and then there was 5000 acres out here that I had the chance to get a hold of for practically a song.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And I went over to the courthouse and it was Saturday and the woman that was in there and she got the books down and says yes she says it's old but you can buy it if you want it. Well I says I want it. Well she says I can't make the deal right now but she says you come over next week and she says we'll make the deal. She says you can buy it. Well what she do, I know just as well in the world what she done. She stepped to the phone and called Hanson up at Potlatch, the land commissioner for the Potlatch you know and he come up. He come up there on a Monday, that Monday and he bought $66 ,000 worth of back tax ground.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And so I went over a couple days after that in that same week I went over and I said well I come over to get that ground, we'll get it fixed up.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And she got the ledger, a great big book, that square you know and she brought it out and she says that Hanson at Potlatch at Lewiston come up and he bought an awful lot of this ground. Well I looked on the ledger and Potlatch, Potlatch, clear all the way down on the ledger and on the next sheet. Potlatch, Potlatch, Potlatch. Well I says my land that's Monopoly. Oh no, no she says not Monopoly. Well it certainly is. I says this ground was supposed to go up for a 30 day bid publicly. I says that's a public domain. Well she said we advertised it and I says what papers you advertised in. Oh we didn't advertise in the papers. She says we just put it on the books only. Well I says you can't do that. Legally I says that's a Monopoly. Now I says here they've gone through here and they've bought, yes she says they bought $66 ,000 worth of back tax ground. Well I says I told you it was a Monopoly. And I says you didn't advertise it publicly at all. And I says somebody stepped in the phone and called him up and let him know that I was coming up here to get a lot of this ground. Well she says I don't know. She says it looks bad alright. Well I know what she done. She stepped there and she was in. I don't know whether they got a cut out or not.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you try to do anything else about it?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: No no no I was working the head mechanic for the Potlatch here. I didn't dare to. I had to lost my job. Although Rich Armour on September when I started to hold out he says buyers, he says I'll have to charge you $2 .00 for every 8 ,000 feet that you bring out off of that timber off over the road.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is it a Potlatch road?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Well he tried to pass it as a Potlatch road but I says let me tell you something Rich Armour. I says you're the superintendent of the Potlatch but you're not running the whole fair. I says that road before the Potlatch ever dreamed of having any ground in there. In 1911 and 12 I hold hay off of those metals on the county road. And I says old Bloom lived up there and several families lived up there and they, the county took care of the road. And I says that's the county road. You rocked it and then claiming it for a road of your own. Well he says I'll have to charge you $2 .00 a thousand. He says that's to help maintain the road. Well Hanson come up a couple days after that and I jumped onto him. Boy I was aureaide.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You were what?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: I was aureaide. I was mad. I jumped onto him. And he says buyers he says that son of a bitch ain't running this Potlatch yet. And he says anything comes into my office. That bill comes into my office he says it'll go right into the waste paper basket. He says that's the county road and he says he knows that. And he says he's got no right there. But he was a hundred percent Potlatch man you know. That is everything was for the Potlatch.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And one thing like that and then the mother I just got out of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When was it that you was doing this about what period of time was it?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Oh it must have been about ten years ago. All that recent. Yes I had two million feet of timber up here on Sam Fries old holdings. And he let it go back for taxes of three hundred and thirty five dollars back taxes on it. I went over there to buy it and she wouldn't sell it to me on that day because she was the only one there. Oh this was that same land. Yeah. Yeah Sam Fries. And then. It was only ten years ago. It seems awful recent for a guy to let it go for that little in tax money. Yeah. That's just nothing. Yeah it was only about ten years ago because I don't think it was ten years ago.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember this bank robbery at Troy?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Oh yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well how long ago was that?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Well now there's been more than one there. Well I wanted to get thirty thousand dollars and they lived over here at Clarkia.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there a few guys that did it or was it just one guy?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Just one guy. Yeah it wasn't that long ago. It was about the same time. It was the same time as he done that. I suppose it's about right. Yeah about ten years ago.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well is that you know in the early days was back around the you know in the twenties and thirties and in there was it was it the same deal if that guy wanted to try to cut you want to get timber?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: If you the Potlatch never cared at all. If you wanted to go out and cut seeds as long as you cleared up the ground and the forestry didn't care.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean they didn't care back then?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: No.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They didn't care if you competed with them?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: No. I mean they let you. Yeah now I went and seen the forestry back in those days and got a permit up here on Coker Meadows to go in and cut old cedar trees you know that was old. But I had to clean up the ground and have their inspector come in after I got through and they'd come in and subay the ground. It was alright that I'd cleaned it up and it was fine and dandy. But when these cedar fellas made a market for it and these big outfits seemed that there was money in it by they took over.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well this land that you wanted to get like on Fry's Place was that mostly cedar?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: No no that had lots of cedar on it but it was nice big sawable timber. I had half a million feet of Tamarack on there that was four feet through. It's still standing. They bought it but they'll never touch it unless it's years and years and years from now.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And now if they would take these big companies the more they get the more they want. I don't know it's many out for money. Now during World War I or Depression in 33 the Potlatch shut down up here and they throw away hundreds of thousands of dollars worth of equipment. Well the Chinese over it, Seattle, Spokane, Portland got in here and they'd fight over it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: The Chinese. Jews. Junk dealers. And they had several fights over it. The Potlatch just throwed away and said if you want anything my now is the time to take it. They wouldn't even, they had $25 ,000 worth of bull blocks and they'd gone out of stock and they just throw them out of the warehouse and they was a pile of half as big as this house. And then they had gas engines and they had tractors. The old, old, best tractors. And they told the people if they wanted any of that stuff to take it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How come they threw all that stuff out? Didn't they think they was ever going to log again?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Not up here. No one. The donkey dogging had gone out and they had no use for the blocks. But during the Depression they went broke. And they borrowed a million dollars of the government to start up on. Today I guess they're number two or three richest company in the United States. But that shows now automation had part to play in that too. They took, well like these scrapples and things. I had luck to do that and burn house and sell us. We all made these scrapples and things for them. And machines, now they're automatic loaders. I made the first one that they ever had. They still got it down here. And put it out and they was loading 200 ,000 feet a day off of this landing down here. But they had two Che engines. And then they had, well there was ten men on the Che's. And then they had five men on the landing, each loading crew, on the landing. And then they had the Marion engineers tied on them. And I made this machine. I had a twin disk motor in it. And they done one machine, done all the work that all the rest of these was doing. And saved them 20 cents a thousand over what they'd been loading for. Well that was starting at the, and then when they...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well when was that, do you remember?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Yes, that was in May. Well that was in the 40s, 44.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well all this equipment that they threw away in the 30s now, who did get that? Local people didn't get any of that stuff?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: No, there was local people that got lots of it. But the moment it's short, yeah, it all went to what I call the Sheenies, these junk dealers from all over the country. Now Block, one of the biggest in the Northwest junk dealers, he got it with a carload. He had a machine that came in there and had a big magnet, and he'd pick it up and set it into the gondolas. He got the most of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would he just use it for selling, what would he do with all that?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Oh he...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would he sell it now for other people?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Oh yes, he sold it all over the country. He bought these shays and rod engines and things at the Potlatch. They sold it at junk price, probably around $40 a ton. I cut up five shays, one rod engine, and Block cut up the rod engine.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And Marion's had six Marion's. Then they cleaned the warehouse out, and down here, old equipment that they had for old -time equipment that they couldn't use anymore. And they throwed out the pile as long as from here over to Maine's house over there, and oh it was piled up as high as this room. Hundreds of thousands of dollars worth of brand new stuff that they just throwed away.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: But of course they had it under contract with Block then. Block bought all their scrap metal. I think they was payoff in there, but you could never prove anything. Well if there's a lot of money involved, you can figure there might just well have been.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So did local people get what they wanted out of that stuff?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: No, no. During the depression times, I don't imagine there was much money to buy that kind of equipment around here. No, the local people couldn't get it. The local people, like Ed Hall, he was a little log in outfit here. When Emory Hall first started out logging, there was some truck bunks that were thrown out into the scrap pile. Well, they cost $150, $200 to make them. And Ed had come up and he'd seen them and he went to them and he says, I'll buy those truck bunks. He says, I can use them. He says, I'd like to have them. I'll buy them. I'll pay good money for them. No, no, I can't sell them to you. He says, we're under contract to Block. And after they go into the scrap pile, that's it. They wouldn't cater at all. A hall was logging against them, you see, to start with. They had two log in trucks, white log in trucks, with the trailers and all the log in harness and everything for $500 down here. And I heard of it. And I went to Joe Parker and I says, Joe, I want those two trucks. Well, he says you can have them, buyers. He was the superintendent then. And he says, I'll fix it up so that you can get them. He says, they only want $500 for the two of them. And my wife says, I want them.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And buyers, my oldest son, went out and got to talking with Parker. He says, well, Dad has got up here on Beulah's Butte, this side of Beulah's Butte, he says he's got a bunch of timber that he can get a hold of. He says AP Lewis was going to buy it. And then he didn't. He moved out here. And he says, he told Dad about this timber. And he says, Dad has got the first choice on it. And he says, we want to log it off. Bring it in salt to the Potlatch. And in an hour's time, Parker come in and he says, buyers. He says, I don't think the Potlatch wants to sell those trucks.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, I says, why?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Well, he says, I don't know why, but he says they don't want to sell them.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And there was a fellow down on Orofino come up the next day and got to talking with him. He says, I just bought these two trucks $500. And he says, I'm going to fix one up and sell it. And he says, the other, and I want for myself. He says, not much to do to them, but he says, I'll go over and fix them up. Well, I says, let me know what you get out of the truck. I says, I tried to buy them, but they wouldn't let me. And he did. He got $5 ,000 for the one truck and kept the other for himself. Well, the Potlatch wasn't a week after that. Johnny Zaglow, the master mechanic here, come in. He says, buyers. He says that timber he was telling about up on Bevel's Butte there. That you could buy it cheap. He says the Potlatch bought it. Well, Parker went and he went over my head, you see, and had him buy that ground. Rich Heimer done the same thing with Sam Fries up here. He had four university surveyors, cruisers, rather, come in. They cruised that timber, and they went through it in day and a half, you know, the four of them. And they marked every tree.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was this on Fries Place or Bevel's Butte?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Fries Place. Well, they'd done the same thing on Bevel's Butte, too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When did this business happen with the trucks and Bevel's Butte? Was that in the 30s, or was that more recent?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Oh, that was in 46, 47. I was logging out on the horse ranch at the time running my crew. I was working in the shop here. I'll tell you, it sure sounds like you'd better off not trusting those guys to the bar. Now, you can't trust anybody as far as getting hold of anything like that. They bought Sam Fries Place. They bought that on that back -tax sale, you know, 66 ,000. Now you go into the county and any timber that they've got, they'll go out and cruise it. And you pay $40, $50, $60, $1 ,000 for every, the stumpage. You got no chance. Little fellas got no chance now at all. Well, buyers, I'm wondering, I still wonder more about, in the early days, what it was like.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now, what about guys, you know, back before the Depression and in there, when they, when, weren't there some guys that were independent outfits and were logging on their own?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Oh, yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now, what did they run into for Potlatch? Did Potlatch care back then?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Oh, the Potlatch would buy their timber, but they wouldn't get nothing for it. They just made 11, that was all. They'd, well, now Sam Fries, up here at Collins. That was, that was back in, oh, 13, 14. And he had white pine in there that'd go four foot across the stump.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And they, they'd give him $4, $1 ,000 to cut his timber and haul it into the landing. And they'd load it. And they'd charge him six bits of $1 ,000, I think, at that time for loading it. And he thought he had the world with the tail. At $4, $1 ,000. Well, you, you take today, one tree would be more than they'd, those nice big white pines that way. I guess they're probably $150 a thousand now. And, uh...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How much would you get in one of, how many thousand would you scale?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Well, those trees averaged over 2 ,000 feet. They were some trees that'd go 3 ,000.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: But, uh... They, they got this whole country in here, practically, for two and a half and three dollars an acre at that time. Well, there was no way that you could, uh, that you could get around selling it to the Potlatch back then. Oh, yes. They, the homesteads, lots of them homesteaded ground here. And, uh, but they'd sell out to the Potlatch. The Potlatch would go in and buy them out. And give them a little of nothing and they'd sell out. Lots of them had to even stay on the place. An old helmer'd go out and... ...cruise it and the Potlatch would buy it. And they'd have to stay and prove up on their place and then they'd move out. But the money was hard to get at that time. So the Potlatch would buy it from them after they'd proved up. Yeah.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Now, the Potlatch had a bunch of timber that they, on old Jones, quit the Potlatch here and went east on the oil deal. I... He'd come back and they had a bunch of timber and they says, we've got a bunch of timber, you're just the man we're looking for. And, of course, OTP was the superintendent for years and years. They had men all knowing. And they says, we'll turn this round over to you and when it gets... It'll be proved up on in a little while and he says, then you can turn it back to us. He says, all right. There was some gipo timber dealers back over in Montana there. They wanted to buy the timber and they give him 25 ,000. I guess they give him 40 ,000 for the timber. No OTP's order to them. Potlatch sued him, well, till he up and died. They still had a case to get. Well, it doesn't sound like they were... that Potlatch would have too good a case since he was improved up on it. It was his land. Yes. They were just trusting him, that was all. But they tried every conceivable way in the world and he didn't dare to keep any money in the bank down here. He had it all up in Canada.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what happened with him? How did he get in trouble on the oil business in Montana?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Well, old Stapleson put the first thousand dollars into Montana there and Jones went in with him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was Stapleson a Potlatch man too?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Well, yes, he was an old logger of the Potlatch and he went in with him and all of the foreman's and everybody that wanted to could buy stock, you see. And we all bought stock.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And they commenced buying up this oil land, these oil leases, and we owned... After we got started, we owned 90 % of the Keevan Sunburst Oil District. And Jones, we got to be worth three million dollars. And Jones, he got to talking with some oil men back east and he watered the stock then. He went and watered the stock and he went back east. But when he watered the stock, lots of these fellas had sold out. Sold there and they sub them for a thousand dollars. They got 25 ,000 dollars for it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That much? It was increased that much, huh?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Oh, yes. And of course, Dad and I, Dad would mine with Jones for 30 years, 35 years as a partner and we stuck with Jones. Well, he went back east and he got in with those big oil outfits and they got him tied up and they broke him. They took over the oil field and everything. Well, he came back and that's when he bought this timber. That is, he sold this timber. He didn't have any. He was dead broke. And when he stepped off of the Shelby there by the Potlatch Man Mag and turned this round over to him. Well, the old teepee was just shooting up then that he sold it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he hold on to it until he pulled it up before he sold it?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: I suppose he did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He held on to it and when the Potlatch come to get it, why did they find out he sold it?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Well, this oil now, had it gone up to, it went up so high.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It went up so high because he watered it, right?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Well, before he watered it. If he hadn't have watered it, all of us fellas stuck with him. We'd been well to do today because them oil fields are still going. They brought in one gush there on the Howling Ranch that was, I guess, a 10 ,000 barrel well. It's still going yet today.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now what is it that happens? What do you do when you water stock? What does that mean?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Well, it was only 100 ,000 shares, you see, he had to start with. Well, he watered it. In partnership proposition, he was just the same as the king. It would be his word that he could do anything he wanted to. Well, we trusted him, you see. And of course, I found that out later, years, that he was the same as the king, but he knew it. He'd had lawyers and things that he knew it. Well, he put five million shares out onto the market, you see. He watered the stock and these big oil companies. Well, they had men working right in there, and they knew what it was.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And before you know it, he was tied up and broke. And we got nothing. I did sell. Dad and I sold, and we got 10 cents on the dollar, I think.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How much had people invested in it? I mean, did you guys have much tied up in it?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Oh, we had, each individual around here had $1 ,000, $1 ,500 in on it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did many sell before? Did many have a profit on it?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Oh, yes. There was quite a lot of them that sold.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And I had to sold when I got to got 12 1⁄2 to 1.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: That's when a lot of them sold at 12 1⁄2 to 1. For every dollar they put in, they put in $1 ,000, where they'd get $12 ,500 out of it. Some of them got more than that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was this after the Depression or before?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Oh, this is before the Depression. Did your father ever talk to T .P.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Jones about it? About what he'd done to him?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: If somebody stole $1 ,500 of my money, I don't know what I'd do, but I'd be mad. Yes. Well, T .P. explained to Dad. Dad was a mining man. He was a mineralogist and geologist.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And he understood a lot of that stuff, and he had perfect confidence in old Jones. If he hadn't of got tied up in the big companies, gotten a hold of him, we'd all been rich here because we'd had that stock. Well, I've got a lock box upstairs. It's full of that stock.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: But when it went down for nothing, we was sold out. Well, did your father still like T .P.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Jones after that?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Oh, yes. He did. That never changed, Dad. He'd been into the mining game.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, was the idea that Jones himself wasn't so guilty, but he'd been used by the company?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Jones himself meant all right. There's no question to T .P. He meant all right, but I'll tell you, when you get tied up with them big, big fellas and everything, where they get you where you can't move, they got you. They'll break you. That's what, when I had this copper mine up on Butyr, I had the Anaconda engineer. And he come up and he spent, oh, he spent all the afternoon with me. Simplot had him out here when Simplot first started.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And he told me, he says, when you deal with these big companies, now he says, I know what I'm talking about. He says, I've handled hundreds and hundreds of cases for this big Anaconda copper company. And he says, I'm still handling. And he says, I get big money. Tell them just what I told you. He says, I get big money for that. And he says, before you get their man to come in here, these big outfits, he says, you have everything tied up perfectly legal and know that it is legal. He says, go to a lawyer and have it drawn up to where it's absolutely foolproof. Man, bring your man in. Well, I had a big outfit from Sheridan, Wyoming. They flew in biplane. Well, they had to go to Elk River to land. And then I met them with Carr and brought them back and took them up there. And they was going to invest $100 ,000 in diamond drilling it.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And I had it so that they couldn't get a hold of nothing.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And they surveyed it and they give me great prospects. He says, there's no question to it. He says, you've got a good thing here and a big thing. And he says, we'll come in and we'll diamond drill it. And we'll know just exactly what you got. But he says, to do that, you'll have to sign papers that we get 7 -8s and you get an 8th. And I says, well, I'm willing to do that. I knew I'd been in the mining game and oil game enough that I knew that an 8th royalty was a big royalty.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And so this big outfit down in Oregon, there was 40 acres down there. They heard it over the television, the big discovery. And they left the next day. I took them to Elk River and they got on the plane. They told me they was going to Oregon to this big strike. And see what they could do down there and they'd be back. Well, I took them to Elk River and they took their plane. They had a private plane. And they went to Oregon. Well, they showed that up then. The economy got that 40 acres there, open pit mining. That's one of the biggest in the West. They never did come back to me. Well, later on in the mining game, I found out that the mining done the same thing. They'd come in, look over your property, investigate it. And with the copper mine and 90 % of the other mines, the little fella can't hang on to it. Because the government demands that you do so much assessment work on every claim every year. Well, a little fella, like I was, with this mine up here, the mine's setting up there today. But now these big companies, when they get short of copper, well, they'll come around to these places and they'll just relocate it and go to work. They've got it for nothing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you let it go?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: No, I couldn't keep it. I had eight claims that it'd be $1 ,000 a year that I'd have to put in. Now it'd probably be $2 ,000 or $3 ,000 a year.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it Anaconda that came to check your mine out?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Oh, yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But when was that? Was that in the 50s?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: No, that was in, yes, that was in... Oh, that was in...

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: That was about 48.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: But that's the way they work now. I was in the Osage Oil Company field. When they made that big strike, you know, I went in and got oil riggins and stuff to bring to Montana.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And they... People who shared in Wyoming, these farmers, every one of them, had natural gas on the places. Well, the country is full of natural gas there. And they had a 3 -quarter water pipe out in their barnyards, and they had a little valve up on top, and they'd light that, and they'd burn it 24 hours a day. It never cost them a dime, the community that way.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And they burned it into their homes and never cost them a dime. And they're still doing it, I suppose. But I found out, this Army captain I was with, they come in, these farmers, they heard that we were there, and they come in and turn over the whole ranch. And some of them had oil on it, but there wasn't big oil strikes there at all. It was only probably a 5 -10 barrel oil well. Well, it cost from $50 ,000 to $100 ,000 to put down a well.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: But they offered to turn over their ground for 12 .5 % royalty to Burke, Oates, and I, because they thought we was big oilmen, wasn't at all. But that shows you, the crooked work. I says to old Cap, I says, well, land sakes, Cap.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I says, how do you expect to handle that ground? I says, if you take over their leases, make leases with them, how do you expect to handle it?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: He says, my boy, he says, you leave that to me. He says, that's the most simple thing you've ever seen. Well, I says, I'd like to know. Well, he says, I'll go to the Ohio Oil Company, and I'll go to these oil companies and say here, for 12 .5%, I'll turn these leases all over to you. And he says, I got 12 .5 % through, clear and above board without investing in nickel. Well, he was a banker. This old captain was a banker in New York, and during the Depression he went broke. He lost, they lost $10 million back there in the bank. Some of the bankers committed suicide over it, and he just laughed at them. And he says, I had a man committing suicide because he lost his life's holdings.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did he do that? Did he take those leases over, Captain?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Oh, he took over some, I guess.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where is the South Sage? Where was it?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Wyoming there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When was it that you were in there with him?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Well, that was in, oh, that was in the 30s. That's when the Potlatch sat down here for a year, and I went to work for the, on the side with old burcos in the oil field.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, he would get 12 .5 % and then what would the farmers get?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: 12 .5%, that's what they offered to us for.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They would get 12 .5 % of what? Of what he got?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Yeah, no, they'd get 12 .5 % of their oil.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And he'd get 12 .5 % too?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Yeah, they had 25%. The oil companies took it over 75%, you see. Well, there wasn't a nickel invested, and these oil companies, they could get a hold of it, and they'd, if they got it well, you never knew it. They were out south of Morcroft, Wyoming there. They're gas wells that, at that time, years ago, that they got afraid to even open them up. Because they was howling and screeching so that they, they was afraid they couldn't have anything to hold it, and they plugged the holes. Poured them full of cement and pulled out from them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This, the copper that you found, was it up on, in the hoodoos or where?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Well, it's, no, it's up on just north of Abe's job here on the back end of Beals Butte. It's wide open today. Did, Bob Olsen tried to, tried to beat me out of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The Olsen brothers from Troy?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Yeah, Bob and Henry. They, they was crooked. They, they never did. Well, Bob, he pulled all kinds of crooked deals all over the country. But I had known them ever since they were young fellows, and I didn't think that they'd, but they tried to jump it on me up here. And I took after Bob once on it, and he never, never went back there. If he did, he never let me go.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, is this, is this the Olsen?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: There's quite a few brothers in that family. There's a bunch of Olsen's around there. I knew what, there's, they're from. Henry worked, he died just a few years ago. You remember he bought the old, where the Moscow post office is now, the old building. And he wanted to hold them up. He bought it for a thousand dollars, and he, he wanted to hold them up for twenty -five thousand dollars on it. But they went and looked the records over, and they, they wouldn't take it. And they told him if he was willing to, they was willing to give him some on his investment, but they wouldn't pay any money like that.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And he sold it to him, but he's dead now too. Bob died.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: I was going to ask you about that, the copper chief that, that your father was invested in.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Wasn't that the name of the mine, or what was it, or was it another one?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Copper king. Yeah, copper king. Yeah. That's still open too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did, did, did he make much money on that when he was buying it?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: No. You told me how afterwards some kids made a lot of money, but. At that time they sent a carload of hand -picked ore over to the Tacoma Smeller, and they, they had it estimated it is running seventy -five, sixty to seventy -five percent on the assays. Pure, it was pure. I've got some of it right here. My stomach is acting up on me. Sixty percent copper. You can take a torch and put on that, and the copper will just run right out of it.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And they, it was a corporation, you see, they sold stock. And then they commenced to lose faith because it was back in there twenty miles in the mountain road going, wasn't a road like they've got in there now. It was up, up on top of the mountains and down, and it had cost them, I don't know what it cost them, I don't remember now, for hauling the, this fellow, Harvard had an old Model T truck. At that time he just bought it, and he could haul a ton at a time. Well, they hauled two -car load, and it cost them, well it cost them plenty to haul it, but they had this hand -picked ore, and they hauled it to Harvard and loaded it out into the gondola center.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And after they'd had it for a while, a dad got a letter back saying, ore runs five percent, isn't worth handling. Dad told him they didn't have any more of this high grade, and he says you owe us a thousand twelve hundred dollars, or twelve hundred dollars for the freight, we had to pay the freight. And he says, your company owes us twelve hundred dollars, and then he says, another, I forget what it was, for handling the ore. Well, they dropped it, and the stockholders, when dad made it known to them, why they all commenced dropping out, well dad kept it for three, four years, and paid eight hundred dollars a year to do the assessment work, he'd hired the man himself, him and Jones, to work it, work out the assessments, and then he'd be able to do it. And then dad towed it up. Well then this pile, they had the pile low as big as this room. And you told me this is the one where the college kids found it, and made money off it. You told me that. And they, of course, the logging road then was up the valley, and perfectly smooth road all the way into it. And they just went up to this stockpile and loaded up two carloads of it, and they got thirty thousand dollars.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What you said now, the first two carloads, that was high grade, didn't they make good money off of that?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: They didn't get nothing. They owned Tacoma Smelter twelve hundred dollars. But I thought you said the Smelter said it was only five percent, but it was sixty percent, you see. Well, yeah, I suppose.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And that's the way they beat the little fella out of the money. You see, Tacoma Smelter, they found out later that a lot of B .C. copper ore come in there, and they beat them out of every bit of it. The Smelter was making the money, but the mines wasn't even clear in there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Didn't they have any proof on that?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: I mean, if they had good ore, they were getting cheated. No, at that time, they never paid much attention to that stuff.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did they know at the time they had sixty percent, or did they just money it?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Yes, they know it. They had a mineralogist come in, and they... I think he's done. The way they take an assay on a bunch of ore that way, they'll take probably five hundred pounds, and they'll pound it all up. And then they'll put it on a blanket, and then they'll walk in the center and lift the blanket up, and they make four quarters of it. And then they take that quarter, and they pound it up and do the same thing again. When they get through with it, they've got an actual value of what the whole thing will run. Well, that's what they've done. They knew what it was. Well, I would think that with some of the brass like T .P. Jones involved in it, they'd have a chance to do something about it, maybe have some influence. You'd think T .P. was just like all the rest of us, working for a living. But you had no chance to get it. That's what I say. It's a little fella with a copper mine. You've got no business monkeying with a copper mine. Because now this mine here, I talked to these mining engineers from the end of the condo, and they told me that it'd take... Well, he says it'll take a hundred thousand dollars to even drill it. Then he says it'll take, to open it up, probably two hundred thousand dollars.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And then he says, time we get a railroad in here to ship it out?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: He says, we've got a half a million dollars or more invested into it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what chance has the little man got at that?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And the government, I went to the government and told them I wanted to put a road in there. And there's five miles of road. And I wanted to ride away over there, round, to put this road in. Fine, fine. I'm glad that you're doing this. But he says now the government calls for a sixteen -foot road bed, steel culverts in all the creeks, and bridge them. And the timber that you cut to put the road in has got to be taken care of. We've got to salvage it. And he says you'll have to pay for the timber that you cut. Well, I says if I had that kind of money, I wouldn't be in here trying to put a road in here. Well, he says that's the government law. And he says that's all we can do. He says we'll give you a permit, all right. But he says you've got to comply with our rules. And it is. We'll have men there to see that it is. Well, I come back and I, in the wintertime I hired two bulldozers, went up there and bulldozed the road into it so I could go up to it with a car, with a four -wheel jeep. And the next spring, the forestry commenced to use the road. And the logging company uses the road for, oh, four and a half, five miles in there where I went before I turned off to go up in onto the mine. Now, a few years ago, I went up there and the water had come down. The road is just a dirt road. And washed gullies in there, because of this house.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: You know, talking about T .P. Jones, I'd heard that when he came back here, and it was, you know, in his later years there that Mrs. Jones wore the pants and doled out the money when he wanted it. Yeah, she handled all the money.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Well, like when I had that coal claim, placer claim over on Swamp Creek. I was coming down from cleaning up, and I had two gallon pails full of the cleanup.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And he come in onto the bulldozers. I happened to be coming down from the mine.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And I told him where my claim was. He talked a while, and he says, if you've got any gold here, buyers, he says I'd like Margie to see what gold looks like in the raw. I said, yes. I said, I've got my samples. I says, I've got gold all through it.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And he'd come down with me, and T .P. I took a handful like that, put it into a gold pan, put it down, and I had, oh, I had stringed gold that wide, about that long right through the pan, you know.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And she just went wild. T .P. says, well, buyers, he says, I've got a map of the art holdings. He says, I own all of this ground in here. But he says, I've got a map here. And he says, I'd like to know where your property is. Well, I looked at the map, and I showed him. And they had it all encircled in red. Well, down to Orofino, they'd made a mistake, you see, and they took in more ground than he had. Somebody in there, they didn't know what they was doing.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And he had it in his ground. He says, don't pay no attention to that, buyers. He says, you go ahead and work that. And he says, I hope you make ten thousand dollars. He says, I've got plenty of ground down here, and don't worry about ever having to get off of it. Well, I knew very well at that time I'd have to put in probably a thousand fifteen hundred dollars to get it on a good fan base. And I got to thinking it over, and I walked away from it. And then in 1933, the forestry come to me and wanted me to take and run camps for them. So I went into the forestry.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of camps? CC camps?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Yeah, CC camps. I handled two hundred and fifty CCs in different camps throughout the time that I was with the forestry.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And I was in the forestry up on Elks Butte. I put in a big dam there for them and put in the water system for the camps. And they took pictures of it and everything else. The finest water system in the CC camps when they were here in the Depression had, I had water just as nice water pressures you had in the cities. I could shoot water, stand here at the road, and I could put water there across with an inch and a half hose there across the road out there. I had that much pressure.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And I had about seventy -five eighty pound pressure.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And I got to talking one night with one of the foresters, foreman's, and I was telling him about this deal with TP and they're over on Swamp Creek. He laughed and he says, I'll tell you something, buyers. He says, old Jones thought he really owned that ground. But he says, I'll tell you something. He says he had a severe come in there and surveyed their ground and your ground wasn't even honest at all. It was this side of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And he says, he come into the bunkhouse and he paced up and down and he says, Smaltz, he says, do you know anything about locating the mine? Mining claim?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Smaltz says yes. He says, I know how to locate a mining claim. Well, he says, I want you to go up and locate that claim. He says, Byers Anderson owned it, but I thought it was mine. But he says he hasn't done anything for two or three years about it. And he says, I want you to go up and locate it for me. So he went up and he located it for old Tom.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And that's the way I got beat out of that claim. Well, you let it go because you thought it was Jones's. Well, yes. Papers right from Orofino that it was included in his ground. Well, I knew if I'd go ahead and get it opened up and everything and went to making anything big out of it, Jones said, well, the company says I'll have to put you off of here byers. Well, that's what he did on too, because they wasn't making anything on their ground down there because they put in a steam shovel and they put a bunch of greenhorns in there and they didn't know how to handle the dirt at all. They just scoop it up with the steam shovel and run it on. They had water into a big cage and run the water through there and they made expenses, but they didn't get anything big. Their fine gold was all getting away from them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was this the company really that had the, it was a Jones that had the money?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: No, it was a company with Jones. Oh, I see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The company being the Potlatch or his mining company?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: It's individuals. You see, they,

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: old Jones never intended to ever work the mine to make anything. He was selling stock and feathering his own nest. And I suppose he made a lot of money on it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This was a different deal than the oil wells though, right?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Oh, yes. This was after he got out, didn't, out of the oil field after they broke him and the Potlatch was after him. He couldn't go back and work for the Potlatch.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This was back in the 20s, right? When he did the oil well business, wasn't it?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Yeah, I think it was. When you picked up that Placer claim on Swamp Creek. That was after 1933.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: There had been a history of people mining on Swamp Creek for years before that, hadn't there?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Oh, yes. The McGann Brothers. The McGann Brothers come in there. They owned all of the Swamp Creek gully where we were. They owned all of that. They worked their ground every year. McGann Brothers.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now is that related to the McGann and Troy?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: No, I don't think so. They come in and they'd take a half gallon fruit jar. And when they got each one of them, the brothers, the two of them, they got it full of gold. Each, the two half gallon fruit jars, they'd go to California and put in the winter. Then they'd come back in the spring and stay in the summer up there and work their claims.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: These would be gold nuggets they'd get in?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Oh, yes. They're little gold nuggets. Some of them run all the size of a bean, popcorn.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they do quite well in there then?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Oh, they made thousands of dollars. In the Jericho mine, in the ledge up on top of the Jericho there, they took out $25 ,000 in what they called a glory hole. But they turned around and spent the $25 ,000 and fallen up the ledge and never got anything. But that's the way that ledge runs. You may go far near to the road, go down in the ledge, and go right into another glory hole.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is a glory hole just what you call a place that's got a lot of good gold in it?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Yeah, yeah, probably half as big as this room. And they'd get that worked out and then they'd... The ledge had run maybe, sometimes they'd run a half mile before you could find another glory hole.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, how far was that from where you were mining?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: That was just as far near to the Cedar Camp, just up the mountain.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the Groves? Didn't they mine and swamp creek too?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Yeah, they was on Cranberry Creek. And that's a pocketed country. The Cranberry was, of course, their gold and then swamp creek. But it is this ledge without a question up on Jericho that was carrying that gold. And the erosion of time washed it out. I went in on Cranberry and went down that far and under the ground. So many of them, the old prospectors, went to bedrock first. They'd go right down to bedrock. They'd never found a sign of gold. Once in a while they'd find a speck. But the old time prospector that knew that country told me not to go over 12, 14 inches under the ground, and I'd get gold. Well, I went in on the head of one creek there, and there was this tree that blew over. And there was probably a hundred gallons of water that had, in the spring, settled in this hole. Well, I put down a test hole. I went down about that far. And there was more gravel there, and I'd take my shovel and run around in under the rocks on the wall. And I brought out there, just testing the ground, I brought out an old string of gold that long around the gold pan and that wide. Pretty stuff you ever seen. I went in there here six, seven years ago and tried to locate it again. I could never find it. But it was there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why would these guys, like the guy doing these two brothers, the McGans, what happened to them? Why didn't they quit?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: They were old men. The last time they was in there, they were old men then. They were around 80 years old.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And they were still at it?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Yeah, the last time they went out of there was they went to California and never did come back. This was considerably before you ever done your mining there. Oh, yeah. Well, they was there. They come back just before I was there. And the old fella up on the hill, I told the old fella that had the Jericho Mine about it and about Jones.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He says, son, don't you think it? He says, that's your ground and don't you give it up?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: He says, I know that country foot by foot. And he says, you keep it. He says, if you don't keep it, he says, I'd like to have it myself. But he says, I've got so much to do here on the Jericho Mine. He was an old man then. He had whiskers.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you know his name? Who he was?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: I don't remember his name. Probably could get it on the record. Oh, I just was just wondering. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he helpful to you, this guy?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Oh, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he talk to you about mining and that kind of thing?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Yeah, he was. If I had listened to him, I'd have to stay alone.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: By the way, have you ever heard of stories about the Chinese miners up on the, out of Harvard, you know, up on the Palouse? Did you ever hear about them getting killed and all that business?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Oh, yes. Yes, there was a bunch of Chinaman in there. This Ott Slat, during the Depression, when the Potlatch is shut down, I had him as a partner, but I found out he was crooked. He was one of the men that was in there. He was a good gold man. I had a gold property up on Gold Center. We'd done a lot of hydraulic work up there. The old working's in there yet. Get out, work all summer, and get an ounce of gold, probably. There wasn't no gold in there to mount anything.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: But, you see, there in the hoodoos, the Chinaman had his cabin right next to the, along the edge of the mountains. These other white men had to get up there, and they'd throw rocks down on the house. The Chinaman'd come out singing and cussing them in the Chinese language, you know, and then they'd pop some of them off, and others they'd shoot into the house. The Chinaman's all left. They were superstitious anyhow, and that was in the early days.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And the Chinaman's all left. Then old Ed Powers got a hold of one of the Chinaman's that he found his claim was off from the hoodoos there. And when Ed Powers found him, he was out of his head, but he had a buckskin bag, and he had it full of nuggets, biggest of the end of your thumb. And the Chinaman said, lots of gold, lots of gold, lots of gold there, lots of gold. And he couldn't talk very good English, and old Ed kept him for years. And every summer he'd go up in there, and he'd try to get the old Chinaman to get into where he knew the country and locate the claim, you see. But the Chinaman'd get up there, and he'd go crazy. I don't know, me no go in there, me no go in there. And he'd take off, and they'd have to catch him, and they'd go back, and he'd keep another year, come up the next spring. He thought maybe he'd gradually, but the old Chinaman was, if he'd understood the Chinese, they're very superstitious. I wonder why he didn't want to go up in there anyhow. Well, he thought he'd get killed without a question.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did this happen in the early days too?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Yeah, that's when the Hoodoos was in its prime there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know where, or did people say where the Chinese cabin was located?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Oh yes, I know where they all are.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: There was more than one Chinese cabin?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Oh yes, yes, they had, I'd been into trenches that'd go as far from here down to the Cedar Mill there, and they'd only be about that wide, and they'd be as deep as this ceiling. I'd gone down into them and walked right along.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The Chinese had dug them, huh?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Oh yes, piled the rock on each side. There are lots of them in there yet.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They must have done a lot of work?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Oh, they'd done lots of work. And then when the dredge come in, there was a mining man, an outfit that, from Seattle, come over and see me. I was down in the city yard here, working for Billy O 'Mearle for the Potlatch in the city yard there.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And he come in and told me who he was, I forget the company that he represented, and he says, I want you to do something for us. And he says, it's a chance for you to make fifteen, twenty thousand dollars if you want to do it. Well, I says, yes, you better all do it. He says, I want you to go into the hoodoos and get leashes on every one of these old farmers that own the ground for gold dredging. And he says, and then I'll sell them to the company, and he says, I'll make you fifteen, twenty thousand dollars, maybe more. But he says, we want that ground. Well, I says, I'll go over. Well, I put it off for two weeks. I had this address and everything here. For two weeks, and then I went over, and I knew those old fellas that owned the ground, you know. I'd known them since I was a kid that high. And I went in, and the bank up here at St. Mary's, they had a mining outfit. And the bank had got on there the week before and showed up all that ground. And then this big miner that had the dredge, he leased the ground from them. Well, they took about seven million dollars out of there. They didn't let it be known, though.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And they took it out. Of course, the other outfit we lost out. So, I'd have got those leases. I'd have made big money there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you know any of those old -time miners that were in there that had little cabins and were mining them?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: I've heard those guys were real different.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: A few old bachelors, were they?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Oh, there's most old old bachelors.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was it like, those guys?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: They were the finest people in the world to talk to and get along with. But if you crossed them, pulled any dirty work with them, I wouldn't want to go back in there because they'd kill you. They'd... Now, let's see, it was Johnny, no, old Dauphinie. Dauphinie had a cabin, square cabin, a little cabin about as big as this room. He had his kitchen one end and over here he had his bed and his table and stuff was all in the one room. But he had portholes in the side of that cabin, about that big. Maybe three inches or four inches, but four inches, yeah. And he had portholes there. Now, the Copper King, when we first located the Copper King, Dad told me lots of times, he says, every time I go through there, he says, their gun pointed on me. He says, if we don't lock the gate, he had cattle, you know, and we went through part of his ground going up to the mine, Copper King. And the Northups had the same thing. And he says, old Dauphinie's... Yeah, Dauphinie says, if they don't lock that gate, he says they'll never go through here again. And he had that gun trained on them through these portholes. And they'd lock the gate and they'd see that it was locked good.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: But then old Dauphinie'd come up to the mine and sit there and talk. But he wasn't going to have his cattle get out. I heard a story about, I think it was him and another guy named Wagner. Wagner, yeah, old Charlie Wagner. Right. Well, the story that I'd heard was that, I think it was Wagner, went over one day and beat Dauphinie.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You heard that story?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Yeah, I seen it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What happened?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: He beat up on Dauphinie and Dauphinie got his rifle and old Wagner jumped the fence and got into the brush and he meant to kill him. And old Wagner went down and got his gun and they watched for one another for a long time.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And Wagner was out working in his garden and Dad come out of the brush. Wagner run over and picked up his .30 -30 and turned around and Dad walked out in the meadow and was coming right up to him. Didn't have, Dad didn't have no gun. And when he got up, I was forced me out of the road further. He says, Bye gosh, Charlie. He says, You know, he says I was watching you awful close. He says, I was getting ready to shoot you. He says, old Wagner says he's going to kill me. But he says, when I work in the garden here, he says I have my gun right with me. And by gosh, Charlie, he says, he don't want to come down here. Think he's going to kill me because he says, Dad's a laugh. He says, Well, he says, I'm not Wagner. And he liked that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, listen, you got any idea why they started feuding?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Oh, they were crazy. They had been in there for years and they, in the wintertime, they probably had no way in there to visit with them and they were practically crazy. Superstitious. They'd go to town and get a wagon load or a pack horse full of groceries. They'd hold up there all winter. And then the summer, why he'd raise cattle, he had quite a bunch of cattle. That's why he was particular about his gate.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: The campers had come in there and left the gates open and they needed to have to work two, three weeks to get them rounded up and back into the pasture.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know, speaking about superstitious and mining, are you familiar at all with the dowsing that some of the old -time miners used to do?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: The ones that were trying to find gold by maybe a bag of bugs. You know, they had, most of them had a needle in the bottle in the cork. They had alcohol in the bottom of it and they'd shake the bottle and they claimed they'd go over the ground and if the needle had started working like that, why, they was gold there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who would have to start doing what, going back and forth?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Back and forth, yeah. And other fellas had all kinds of contraptions, trying to locate gold, but I don't think any of them ever worked.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you know of the Caracos at all? The what?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: The Caracos, they were up on Gold Hill. No. That wasn't really in the Who -Dos. No, that was over there. I had lots of history on the old mines there.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: I've never really been able to figure out why they disliked the Chinese so much, but I've heard, you know, they disliked it. Well, the Chinaman come in, make money where the white man would start death and they'd get gold. He was much finer. You take the Chinese, one of the finest gold men in the world. Now you take in China there where 90 % of our gold comes from there. They'll take old barrels or anything and make a furnace out of it, and they'll melt the rock out, work on it for days to get a little spoonful of gold. They're the greatest gold men that we've got today. And it's the greatest gold country in the world, because that's where most of the gold comes from. But it seemed like a lot of people really hated them. They just thought they were dirt, you know. Well, yes, they did. They treated them like dogs. You take in on the Salmon River there, they killed. There was one fella that went in there. Gangsters, all he was. And he'd wait till the Chinaman had to get in and work and have their gold, and then they'd go in and hold them up, kill half a dozen of them, and take their gold. And they'd move out, and nobody knew anything about it. And lots of gold in on the Salmon River there, that's the way they operated.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were there any Chinese in Palouse there when you were living there?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Oh, yeah, there was around 700. And most of them all was in that gold field, on Gold Hill, and up in there, and on Moscow Mountain. There, they're gold in on Moscow Mountain there, that the Chinese worked years ago. I guess some of them do a little work on their mines yet in there, I think. I don't know of any that are still around the country. You could probably go in there from the road, I don't know where the road goes up there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the fire that they had here on the 4th of July? You remember that 4th of July fire? You were writing?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Yeah, I was writing here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's the story, what happened to how it started and all?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Well, it was always surmised, and I don't think they was far wrong. Joe Safer, he went to the bank the night before, July the 3rd, and he'd drawn out $1 ,800.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And Joe was a fine fella, he was middle aged, the finest fellas he ever met. And he went up to his room there in the Sherman House, that is the end hotel, the old Sherman House.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And the fire started there. We knew the fire started there. But he was burnt pretty much the time we got in there and pulled him out. And he was face down on the mattress. The mattress was all burnt and his back was all burnt. And he never had a scent on him.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And they, of course, the fire went right through then, and it was uncontrollable. And it took the whole town, the whole block out, block and a half. Took the main street and half of the other street out.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And there's no question somebody knocked him over the head and took his money, and then they realized that they'd killed him, and they set that fire. And I, on the 4th, the morning of the 4th, about 11 o 'clock, when the town had burnt up, on the night of the 3rd, I got on the train to go to Palouse to the celebration, because there was no celebration here. And there was a man and his wife and a young kid about, oh, 14, 15 years old. Well, of course, this young kid, I sidled up to him and made friends with him,

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: and got off at Palouse when I told him I was going to Colfax and celebrate down there and coming back.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And I got to talking with him, and he says, I'd like to go to Colfax. Well, I says, see your dad and get some money if he'll let you have it, and you can go to Colfax with us. There was four of us young fellows together, and I says, you can go to Colfax and come back with us. We're going to get a car and go down in the car. Old Bill Thompson, the policeman there in Palouse, had an old, I guess it was an old studebaker open, no roof on it or anything. We went down in it. Well, that kid, he says, I don't need to see my dad about money. And he pulled his pocketbook out and showed it to me, and he had a stack of 20s about that thick. He could hardly shut his purse in the purse. And he says, if I haven't got enough money here, he says, dad's got all kind of money. And you know, I've often wondered if they wasn't the one that hit old Joe over the head and robbed him, because for a kid 14 years old, I thought of that years later, a kid having that amount of money, and lastly, it was rich people, and then I wouldn't give him that amount of money. He must have had $400 there and $20 bills.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they from Bovill?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: No, they were complete strangers.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they coming from Bovill that day?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Well, yes, they was taking the train, and on the train. I got acquainted with this kid, you see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So did he come with you to Colfax?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Yes, they went down to Colfax, and he spent money like water. They'd get beer and bring it out and give it to him, but he couldn't go into the saloon. And that's when one of the kids who's with me was 24 years old, and I was only 16. And I had a beard, a heavy beard, and went into the saloon there, and the bartender I ordered a beer, and George David ordered the beer, and the bartender reached over and got ahold of George's face, and he says, outside, boy, he says, you get no beer in here. George says, I'm old enough to get a beer, and he says, I'm older than this fella. He says, I said, outside, you get no beer in here, now you get out. He reached over and fell to my chin, and he felt a heavy growth of the whisker, and he had me up here.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: I was over 16, yeah, I was 17, 18 at that time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, listen, that kid and his family, did they disappear out of that?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Yeah, I never knew what would come up after that. Seems to me they said they were going back to San Francisco. If I remember correct, I think they said they were going on their way back to California, and they were stopping in Bloose. They had a realty for something there that they were going to visit. Of course, there might not have been nothing to it, but at the same time, I...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you think of it then? Did you think of it then? Did it strike you at the time, or was it just later?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: No, not till later. I got to thinking of a young kid having that much money, and I couldn't often thought about it. Same way when I was part of that fight up at the Elk River. I went over and told the sheriff, I said, you better get over there. I said, I don't know whether they killed this man or not, but I said, they was 15, 20 of those bow hunks, I said, jumped onto him and stabbed him to death, I think. I said, I got out of there. I said, you better go over, and he went over. But as I remember, the fella that done the most of the stabbing was a little glassy -eyed, ball -headed fella with the mustache.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that the description you gave to the cop?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Yeah, I give him the description, told him. He went over there, and he went all through the logging camps. There was camps all over there, you know, and they couldn't find anybody, and nobody knew anything about a man of that description. But I got back here, and Mike Bubley come up to me on the street,

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: and he grinned, and I recognized him. He's the man that hit him over the head with the gun. He had a big gun, a kitten, he wrapped him over the head and knocked him out, and then old Sam, the man that killed him without a question, because I've seen Sam's plots after that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, you saw Mike Bubley, and he grinned, and did he say anything to you on the street?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Yes, he said I'm going to get out of the country, and he had a bag, and he had saving bonds in the bag, until this thing blows over. Well, I said, no, we used to get out of there if he wasn't in on the killing. But I was just a young punk, and I didn't realize then until later that Mike was implicated there. Because he hit him over the head. Yeah, he hit him over the head with that big six -hooter. And it was an old -fashioned six -hooter, about that long.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Over a foot long?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Yeah.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: When you... Mike says it wasn't Sam that... I guess you told me. Yeah, that's what he said. I've never talked with Mike. I go and see Mike every little while. When I go to Spokane, I go down and visit with him. But... But it was Sam P. Wash, because I played card games with Sam, and he'd get mad. And the first thing he'd done, he'd jerk out a knife about that long. Well, wait a minute.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was Sam the only one that stabbed him, as you remember it?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Well, he was the main one, yeah. There was two, three others just kicking him and everything, but Sam stood right over him with his knife. And you saw him... When you came in, was the ruckus...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Had the ruckus already started up when you came into the place?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Oh, yes. It started up, and I got him away from him once, and I grabbed him and pulled him out onto the porch, and I says, boy, I says, don't tie into them. I says, they're 15, 20 of those fellas in here, and they'll beat you half to death. He says, I can lick them fellas just as fast as they get to me. And they came in there, and they started for him again. And boy, he hit four or five of them, and he just knocked them end over end. And then the whole bunch started in for him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was this place a whorehouse, too? And a place you'd get booze at, too?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Yeah, that's what we went over there for, so we could get drinks.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How come all it was all Bohunks in there?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Well, all of the Potlatch men, the men in the woods, 90 percent of them were Bohunks, foreigners.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And there was donkeys all around there on the flat. And there was three camps right in close there. Of course, that's where they went.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What were they like, these Bohunks?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: I mean, besides that...

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: The 90 percent of them were morons without a question.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Now, old Sam couldn't read or write, and you'd take this other Joe... It wasn't Joe Povitch.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Potlatch Joe?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: No. Well, he was another moron. Potlatch Joe, they'd fire him out of one camp, and he'd go right to the nicks and go to work without being told to go to work. They'd let him work a month or so, and then they'd give him a check. But he'd go into the table and eat like a hog.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why would they fire him out of one camp?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Oh, they'd get tired of him and run him out of the camp, and he'd go to another.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Nogel first took over, went up there, and the railroad crew would get out and snowball it. He'd use Sunday bitches, use Sunday bitches, and they'd done it just tormenting, you know. No, Joe would say, use Sunday bits, use Sunday bits, use Sunday bits. He'd pick up the snowball and they'd grab him and wash his face. He was a good -mannered old fella. He never got mad, but that's the way they were. They were just a scum, I think, those fellas. And when the railroads come in, when the railroads come in, they're close. On the inland, they killed a man right below our place. He'd run into the neighbor's house that lived just right as far as the middle of the road, right below us. This fella run in there and he says, they're after me to kill me.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Can I stay here tonight?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: He says, them fellas are going to kill me, going to kill me.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And Wolsey, Alan Wolsey's granddad, says, yes, you come in and stay. He says, I'll fix you a bed on the lounge here and you stay here tonight and go out when it's daylight. Well, the fella did, but he'd come into town the next night, hung around, and then he started home, and they caught him. They killed him and robbed him, and then they beat it. They didn't know their names. They all signed their names by axis.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he a bow hunt, this guy?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: He was just one of them. Yeah, he was just one of them. And then they fight, get into the saloons there. There were 13 saloons there, and they'd get into the saloons and fight like cats and dogs.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And flus?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Yeah. You'd see them when they'd go back to work. They'd have their head all bandaged up and some of them had black eyes and terrible -looking outfits.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: But even back when I was on the steel gang, I kept time on the steel gang when I first came up here in about 1912.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And them fellas couldn't sign their own name. They'd come in, I knew them by name, and called them by numbers. I had them all numbered by numbers, and they'd come in and buy stuff, and they'd give me their number, and I'd put it down on them. And that's the way they were paid, by numbers.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: They was illiterate.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, how big was the steel gang that you were keeping time for? How big was that?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Oh, they had two, three hundred men.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You were keeping time for that many? Really? Was this all for Potlatch? Was it all for Potlatch?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: They had a steel gang, you see. They had five or six big cars. Well, that's one of them right down here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know, this place that Lord owns down here?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Oh, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That big long building?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: That's one of the old steel gang buildings.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And they would pull them into different campsites. You went right along with them, you know. And they'd build the logging roads.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: They'd go in, and they'd... It's like going up on Cougar Meadows. They'd go in there, and they'd... Until they built all the roads in there, they wouldn't come out. And then they'd go down in Baderie, go in and build them up in next to the mountains there. These roads now, is this for the chaise, or... This was mostly for the engines, railroad going on. Yeah, it was the railroad. That's what the steel gang was, is the railroad crews. And they never had anything to lift the rails. The men lifted manpower. They'd be 30, 40 men get ahold of these rails, and they'd pack them out. They never had anything that had... Then later on, they got the marians there, and they handled the rails with the marians. And then they'd be four or five bow hunks on each side, and they'd get ahold of the rails with tongs, and they'd slide them up and pull them in on the railroad.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How many men would it take on one rail if they were doing it by hand?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Oh, they'd be... Well, a 30 -foot rail, and they'd be just as thick as they could get there. I'd say they'd be 20, 25 men, ahold of it. And they'd walk right off with them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And the crews were all foreigners on all these crews?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Oh, yes. 90 % of them was. The only white ones was the bosses.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And McFarland was the boss over the steel gang, and then he had Dick K. Meyer, who was the foreman and under him. And then the cooks and flunkies were white people. Well, these others are some of them white, but they didn't. They all talked in their own language. You couldn't understand them at all. Were most of these guys from just... Mike Bubley, he's from Bosnia, or he's from Yugoslavia.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were these guys from different countries? Some from Italy, some from Greece?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: They was... Slovakia, Serbians. Pete Julewicz was a Serbian. And during the war, one, they sent for Pete to come home. He was out of the army there, going to the army. He'd haul it all. Well, he had big holdings. He was worth thousands of dollars in property back there by his folks. Left him. He wouldn't go back and claim it at all. He says, I go back there, I get killed. And then there was a couple big other fellows. One of them was a man about your size, and the other was a great big fellow. And I had an automatic colt, automatic.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And he'd seen it one time. I don't know whether it was target chupiner or something. He'd seen I had that gun, and when the war broke out, they sent for him and Pete Julewicz and a lot of these fellows to come back and go into the army.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
